segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Isabella, a woman with long dark hair in a black blazer, speaking directly to the camera against a bright blue background adorned with animated money bags, stacks of cash, and a laptop. She introduces herself as the owner of two six-figure online businesses and enthusiastically welcomes viewers to her free 'STEP BY STEP Print on Demand Course,' emphasizing that similar courses typically cost over $1000. Isabella, speaking with a clear and persuasive voice, gestures with her hands as she encourages viewers to smash the like button for the YouTube algorithm and subscribe to her channel to support her in making more free courses. A YouTube 'Subscribe' button animation appears on screen. The scene then transitions through a series of quick cuts, illustrating the course's practical applications: a person browsing an online clothing store on a laptop, a delivery van unloading boxes (symbolizing avoiding inventory), and a woman shopping in a boutique. Bold yellow text overlays declare 'THIS IS THE BEST FREE COURSE' and 'START WITH $0', promising viewers will be ready to launch their online store with print on demand. Isabella's narration continues, explaining that the course is for anyone wanting to start a clothing brand or sell products online without holding any inventory. The video then shifts to an animated infographic titled 'How Print On Demand Works,' where Isabella's voice clearly details the three-step process: designing a product and selling it in your online store, a customer placing an order, and the print-on-demand company printing and shipping the order directly to the customer. Animated hands point to each step in sequence as she describes them.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with an animated infographic titled 'How Print On Demand Works,' visually detailing a three-step process from designing a product to a customer ordering, and finally the POD company printing and shipping. A white cartoon hand guides the viewer through these steps, while a female voice explains that the print-on-demand company will handle printing and shipping the product directly to the customer, freeing the user from these tasks. This transitions to a more detailed five-step diagram, illustrating the customer placing an order, the order going to the POD supplier, the supplier printing the item with the user’s artwork, packaging it, and shipping it. The female voice clarifies that the user's primary role is to 'create the design and product,' with the POD company managing 'all the rest,' allowing the user to 'keep the profits.' The scene then shifts to a bright blue 'Benefits' slide with bold yellow text, listing advantages like starting with $0, no inventory commitment, less saturation than dropshipping, fast shipping, and quick setup. Animated dollar bills reinforce the financial appeal as the female voice elaborates on each point, explaining the ability to 'start with zero dollars,' the absence of 'boxes of clothes in your house' due to no inventory, the market being 'a lot less saturated than dropshipping,' and the availability of suppliers with 'super fast shipping.' The final frame presents a 'WHAT YOU WILL LEARN' slide, outlining topics such as designing clothing, creating a logo, and building a POD store, accompanied by an icon symbolizing knowledge and profit. The female voice introduces this as 'what you're going to learn in this free course,' beginning to list the topics, including 'what print on demand really is, how to design your own clothing, how to create a logo.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a bright, light blue animated slide titled 'WHAT YOU WILL LEARN,' listing key topics such as 'WHAT IS PRINT ON DEMAND,' 'HOW TO DESIGN CLOTHING,' 'HOW TO CREATE A LOGO,' 'HOW TO BUILD YOUR POD STORE,' 'HOW TO FULFILL ORDERS & AUTOMATE ORDERS,' and 'HOW TO MARKET & GET SALES.' A female speaker articulates these points, emphasizing how to build a "print on demand store for free, of course, $0 monthly," and how to "market and get sales," concluding with how to "fulfill orders and connect the print on demand supplier with your online store." An icon of a lightbulb over a book and coins, along with text overlays 'FOR FREE $0 MONTHLY' and '& SO MUCH MORE!', visually reinforces the speaker's message about the course's cost-free nature. The scene then transitions to a new slide titled 'HOW POD IS USUALLY TAUGHT:' featuring the Shopify logo and a cartoon cat holding money, both crossed out with red X's. The speaker explains that "usually people that teach print on demand, they just always recommend the same thing, which is using Shopify and buying ads," but she finds "both of these things are unnecessary, especially for beginners." Bold text 'I WILL SHOW YOU HOW TO START COMPLETELY WITH $0' appears, aligning with her personal experience: "I definitely did not have any money when I first started my entrepreneurship journey." The video then shifts to a real-world setting, showing a pair of hands placing a cardboard UPS SurePost shipping box on a wooden floor. The speaker states, "So this is what your customer is going to receive," explaining she ordered samples. The box features a custom label with a dog silhouette and the name 'MEEKA THE HUSKY,' which the speaker identifies as "Mika's merch store." A finger points to the label, and text 'WHAT YOUR CUSTOMER WILL RECEIVE' and 'THEY ALLOW YOU TO CUSTOMIZE SO MUCH!' appear, as the speaker highlights how they "even put your company's name here" and "a picture that they customize on the label."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a cardboard UPS SurePost shipping box, featuring a custom label with a dog silhouette and the name 'MEEKA THE HUSKY.' A female voice immediately highlights the extensive customization options, noting that the label appears to be from 'you' rather than Printful, allowing for personalized branding with a company name and chosen picture. A hand then opens the box, revealing several items neatly wrapped in plastic. The female narrator explains that this is 'what we got' and pulls out a packing slip, clarifying that while it lists purchased merchandise like a white hoodie, black crop top, and a men’s t-shirt, it notably does *not* include original prices. Instead, it features a personalized thank-you message, Instagram promotion, and the customer's company name and chosen image. As the hand removes the items from the box, the narrator praises the packaging, noting that everything is neatly 'bagged' and 'folded,' not just 'thrown in here.' The hand then reveals a white t-shirt with a large, colorful graphic of a husky dog wearing a pink bandana with the text 'FLUFF YOU!' and 'MEEKA' beneath it. The t-shirt is laid flat on a wooden surface, and the female voice enthusiastically points out various design details, praising how 'nicely' the design printed. She explains that she uploaded a file, having originally taken the husky's picture with her iPhone, not a professional camera, and added the words using Printful, reiterating the excellent print quality. Text overlays appear, stating 'I WILL BE SHOWING YOU HOW YOU CAN CREATE CLOTHING LIKE THIS!', reinforcing the focus on the quality and design of the custom apparel and the personalized print-on-demand process.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a screen recording of the Printful dashboard, specifically on the 'Product templates' page, where various existing templates like mugs and hoodies are displayed. A female voice explains the goal is to demonstrate how to make a hoodie, noting that the example is embroidered and 'really nice.' She then instructs the user to 'just click on product templates,' explaining that the interface will display 'all the different products that you can sell in your online store.' The user clicks the 'Create new' button, transitioning to the 'Create a product template' interface, which shows a grid of product categories. The voice confirms these categories, mentioning 'kids, accessories, home and living, like pillows and mugs,' and 'different branded items,' emphasizing the extensive customization options available. Following the voice's instruction to focus on 'men's clothing,' the user navigates through the categories, hovering over options like 'Men's clothing' and 'Hats' before selecting 'Men's clothing.' This leads to a subcategory view with options such as T-shirts, Hoodies, and Sweatpants & joggers, with the voice mentioning the intent to find a 'unisex hoodie' and defining it as 'for men or women.' The user then clicks on 'All shirts,' revealing a list of men's t-shirt products with details like price, reviews, estimated delivery, and available colors. The voice reiterates that 'they have so many different things that you guys can customize here.' The user hovers over several t-shirt options, including the 'Men's Classic Tee | Gildan 5000,' which is marked as a 'Bestseller' with a price of $10.95 and a 5-star rating. The voice identifies this as 'the men's classic tee' and explains its selection, stating it 'costs you around $11 to make it.' The segment ends as the voice highlights that 'the estimated delivery is 5 to 10 days.'

The video then cuts to the Ecwid website homepage, promoting starting an online store for free, while the speaker announces, "Now I'm going to show you how to create your own website/online store 100% free, $0 monthly." segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a screen recording of the Ecwid by Lightspeed online store setup process, as a female speaker encourages viewers to use a link in the description to create a free account. The user is guided through Step 1 of 4, entering 'Online Store' as the store name and confirming 'United States' and 'U.S. dollar', validated by green checkmarks, while a prominent blue text overlay 'USE THE LINK IN DESCRIPTION TO SIGN UP FOR FREE!' with decorative stars is visible. The speaker advises clicking 'Next', then suggests skipping Step 2 (adding a company address, with 'Florida' pre-filled) and Step 3 (providing user and store information), stating these are not strictly necessary. Moving to Step 4, the user is presented with pricing plans: 'Free' at $0/month, 'Venture', 'Business', and 'Unlimited'. The speaker emphasizes the 'Free forever plan' at $0 a month, calling it 'perfect for beginners'. The user selects the 'Free' plan, clicking 'Create a store – it’s free'. The screen transitions to the Ecwid dashboard, displaying a 'Greetings!' message, a 'To-do list' with tasks, and a 17% progress indicator. The speaker explains her reasoning for recommending the free plan, noting that 'nothing blows up overnight' and beginners shouldn't pay monthly before making money, before instructing the viewer to click 'Create a store'.

Finally, the user clicks 'Save'. The interface remains clean and modern, with a focus on user-friendly customization, and the background image of the skateboarder adds a dynamic, youthful energy to the design process.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a screen recording of the Ecwid product management interface, where a user is deleting sample products from their catalog. A female speaker explains, "we don't need them anymore," though she acknowledges the value of sample products for learning. She then instructs, "let's go ahead and just delete all of these." The user proceeds to select 'SAMPLE. Blue Sleeveless Coverall' and 'SAMPLE. Bright Yellow Track Pants,' clicking 'Delete Product' from a dropdown menu and confirming each deletion in a pop-up dialog, with a green '1 product deleted' notification appearing each time. After removing multiple samples, the list narrows to a single remaining product: 'Unisex Faith over Fear Shirt.' The speaker notes that with the samples deleted, only "the product that we set up with our two options, which is different sizes and different colors" is left. The user then navigates to the 'Import or Export Products' section, opening the 'Import Products' dialog to select a CSV file from their computer, choosing 'X-Cart - LifeCommerce' as the import type, with the speaker confirming, "If you did want to import your products through a um CSV file, you can do that here." After closing the import dialog, the user returns to the main Ecwid dashboard, which displays a 'To-do list' with progress at 50%. The speaker guides, "now we're going to just go through these." The user clicks through the checklist, marking 'Review regional settings' and 'Choose how to deliver your goods' as complete, updating the progress to 67%. The speaker clarifies that delivery was "already did that through this one" and regional settings are for selling to specific areas like "the United States." Finally, the user clicks 'Set up payment options,' leading to the payment settings page. The speaker announces, "Now, we're going to go to set up payment options. So go ahead and click on that." Here, the user toggles the 'Pay by cash' option off, as the speaker explains it's for events and not relevant without inventory. The user then views available payment providers, including Stripe and Square, with their respective fees and features, hovering over the 'Connect and Enable' button for Stripe, indicating an intention to set up online payments.

segments.integrated_caption: A screen recording displays the Ecwid website's 'Add new payment methods' page, where a user scrolls through various payment providers like Stripe, Square, and PayPal. As the cursor hovers over Stripe, the voice clarifies that Stripe allows customers to pay with a credit card, emphasizing that the displayed fees (e.g., Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction) are "very, very normal" for processing payments and are standard across platforms like Shopify or Wix. The page further reveals manual payment methods and supported types like credit/debit cards, Apple Pay, Google Pay, and invoicing, all while the speaker advises that these fees are consistent across different website hosts, so users aren't missing out by choosing Ecwid. The user appears to be evaluating these choices, with browser tabs for Tradingview and Coinbase visible, indicating an e-commerce context. The speaker concludes by reiterating her personal preference for PayPal and Stripe.

segments.integrated_caption: The screen recording displays the Ecwid website interface, focusing on the 'Shipping method' and 'Order breakdown' sections, while a female speaker provides advice on e-commerce pricing and shipping strategies. A red notification bar at the top warns, 'Your shipping methods are not available to customers.' The selected shipping method is '$3.99 - Flat Rate' with an estimated delivery of April 5–10. The speaker notes that customers react negatively to shipping costs, suggesting tactics like free shipping or increasing shipment prices to cover costs, or simply pricing the product higher. The order breakdown shows 'Fulfillment' at $12.95, 'File digitization' at $6.50, 'Shipping' at $3.99, and 'TAX' at $1.37, totaling $24.81. The speaker emphasizes the importance of making at least $10 profit per sale, stating, "I would want to see you at least make $10 per sale, at least." She explains that the platform handles much of the work, and the user's role is listing and marketing. The cursor hovers over the 'File digitization' section and the total amount as she clarifies that the displayed total is what the user pays with the customer's money, from which profits are kept. She advises going through each product to calculate shipping costs to set appropriate prices, noting that "everything's a process." A red 'Continue to review' button and a 'Save as draft' link are visible, with the speaker mentioning the latter for iterative work. The Printful logo is visible at the bottom left, indicating the fulfillment service.

The user briefly clicks on 'Dashboard,' which brings up a 'Greetings!' pop-up, identified as part of the Ecwid dashboard setup, showing a completed to-do list for store configuration. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a screen recording of the Zarla logo customization tool, where a female speaker explains, "This is how you could actually make the logo." She notes that the tool offers "a bunch of different things that you can use here," demonstrating how to type 'faith over faith' into the brand name field and select 'Clothing' as the industry. As she highlights the horizontal layout options, she instructs the user to "click on layouts right here" to change the orientation. The interface displays multiple logo templates, and the user selects one featuring a pink dress icon with stars above it, paired with the text 'FAITH OVER FAITH'. The speaker elaborates that "you can customize this however you want to do," showing how to adjust colors and icon options using the left sidebar controls for 'Icon', 'Name', 'Colors', 'Background', 'Layouts', and 'Ideas'. She points out that the tool "even show you like how it would look on the clothing," as the logo is previewed on various mockups including a white t-shirt, business cards, a mug, a tote bag, and a laptop screen displaying a Facebook page, all featuring the pink logo. The speaker concludes this section by stating, "this is what I would play around with if you want to create your own logo to actually upload to the site." The scene then transitions to a product page for a 'Unisex Faith over Fear Shirt' priced at $19.95, showing a maroon t-shirt with a 'FAITH / FEAR' design and floral elements, where the user selects size 'S' and color 'Pink', then clicks 'Add to Bag'. The video concludes with a title card introducing an 'ALTERNATIVE PRINT ON DEMAND METHOD: Etsy', featuring the Canva and Printful logos alongside an animated character typing on a computer, as the speaker begins to transition to the next topic.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a static title card, 'ALTERNATIVE PRINT ON DEMAND METHOD: Etsy,' displayed in bold yellow and orange text against a light blue background. The Canva and Printful logos are on the left, while an animated character with dark hair, wearing a blue and purple long-sleeve shirt, is shown sitting at a desk and typing on a black computer keyboard on the right. A female voice immediately begins discussing this method, stating it's "another popular way that people do print on demand" using "Etsy, Canva, and Printful," with Etsy being where products are actually sold. She clarifies that she doesn't teach it this way and it's not her "favorite way" because "Etsy takes so many fees" even before money is made. She reminds viewers she's "all about starting with zero dollars" and offers to make a dedicated course on Etsy print on demand if requested in the comments, acknowledging it's a popular method many teach. She emphasizes that if you're marketing your own products, you should keep all the profit, but if you create an Etsy store and "they're getting you sales, then that's when it's worth it." The scene then transitions to a new title card reading 'HOW TO GET SALES WITH YOUR POD STORE WITHOUT PAYING FOR ADS!' in bold yellow and white. This card features a real-life video inset of a person's hands typing on a silver laptop, browsing an online clothing store with various t-shirt designs, while a cartoon stack of green money with wings appears on the right, symbolizing free sales. The speaker continues, asking, "Now that you have your print on demand store all set up, how are we going to get sales?" She explains, "This is going to be through the way that you promote and market your online store," and begins to introduce "one of the best ways to promote your print on demand store is by using Pinterest."

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a screen recording of the Pinterest mobile app, where a female voice explains that Pinterest launched "Idea Pins" allowing users to put a link directly into the pin, emphasizing the benefit of promoting through photos. She highlights Pinterest's function as a search engine, where users actively look for content. The screen displays a search for 'graphic tees' with various product pins, including a 'Main Character Energy Oversized Graphic Tee' from Miami Outfits. The user taps on this product, revealing a detailed page with a $45.00 price, white color, size L, and a 'Buy with Shop Pay' button. After navigating back to the Pinterest feed, showing fashion-related pins, the female voice transitions to demonstrating how to post an Idea Pin, mentioning the use of a free business account. The video then shifts to the Pinterest 'Create' interface, where the user, guided by the voice, clicks the plus button and selects 'Idea Pin'. A mock-up photo of a t-shirt featuring a husky graphic with 'FLUFF YOU!' text is chosen. The voice explains that the title section is for keywords, demonstrating by adding 'Husky lovers dog animal' as a title, clarifying it's for a "funny husky shirt" related to the "dogs" theme.
